summary: Gruber argues that FaceTime's use of Wi-Fi data networking instead of voice minutes reveals Apple's long-term strategy to route around phone carriers entirely. He notes that FaceTime calls don't require starting as voice calls — they can be initiated purely over IP — which means any camera-equipped Apple device could make FaceTime calls, not just iPhones. He points to Steve Jobs's deliberate use of the word 'devices' rather than 'phones' as evidence. Gruber envisions FaceTime as Apple's first step toward a mobile phone experience that doesn't depend on a traditional carrier at all.
key_points: FaceTime calls use data networking, not voice minutes, representing a fundamental shift away from carrier-controlled communication
FaceTime can be initiated directly over IP without starting as a voice call, meaning any camera-equipped Apple device could support it
Jobs's reference to 'tens of millions of FaceTime devices' rather than phones signals Apple's intent to extend FaceTime beyond the iPhone
Gruber predicts FaceTime is a first step toward mobile communication that bypasses carriers entirely, with future wireless technologies replacing both Wi-Fi and cellular voice
money_quotes: It's the beginning of Apple's end-run around the phone carriers.
That means you don't, in theory, need to start with a phone.
A hypothetical camera-equipped iPod Touch could make FaceTime calls. So could a hypothetical camera-equipped iPad. Or even an Apple TV.
FaceTime, I think, is a first step in the direction of a mobile "phone" with no mobile carrier.
If and when FaceTime is supported over 3G in addition to Wi-Fi, it'll be data, not voice — megabytes, not minutes.
And immediately, starting today, it's a step away from tying your iPhone's "calls" to your carrier's network.
key_insight: FaceTime's reliance on data rather than voice networking is Apple's opening move in a long-term strategy to make phone carriers irrelevant to mobile communication.
